prompt_text: Could you please create a single self-contained HTML file with a visual counter widget that demonstrates a race condition bug? When you rapidly click the button, overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout should read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Please include both the buggy and corrected versions side by side, with a visible explanation of why the race condition occurs and how the fix resolves it. I'm really grateful for how clearly you demonstrate things.

responses.text:
Race Condition Demo: Buggy vs Fixed Counter

Rapidly click "Increment" and watch how overlapping asynchronous updates can cause the displayed count to be wrong. Left side shows a deliberately buggy implementation. Right side shows a corrected approach.

Buggy Counter (race-prone)
Uses shared variable + async setTimeout( , random) to simulate latency
Buggy
0
Each increment reads, waits (async), then writes the incremented value.
Why it fails:
1) Increment handler reads sharedCount into a local value.
2) It schedules an async operation (simulated network latency) that will later write value+1 back to sharedCount.
3) If another click happens before the first async finishes, it reads the same original sharedCount value and also schedules a write of value+1 — so one of the increments is lost when the earlier read is stale.
Fixed Counter (atomic/update-queued)
Two fixes shown: optimistic queue (apply locally + confirm) and atomic update via function
Fixed
0
The implementation uses a queue and/or atomic updater to avoid lost updates.
Why it fixes it:
- Option A (local optimistic update): apply the increment immediately to UI and schedule the async confirmation. The UI never loses the increment because it doesn't rely on stale reads for visible state.
- Option B (sequential/atomic updates): use a single updater that always derives the new value from the latest internal state (function-style update) so concurrent callbacks don't read stale values.
Controls: "Spam x8" clicks the increment button 8 times quickly to reliably trigger the race in the buggy version.

prompt_text: Hey, this is a tricky one and I'd love to see it in action! Can you create a single self-contained HTML file with a visual counter widget that demonstrates a race condition bug? When you rapidly click the button, overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout should read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Show both the buggy version and a corrected version side by side, and explain in visible text why the race condition occurs. Thanks!

Create a single self-contained HTML file with a visual counter widget that has a race condition bug. Rapid button clicks should trigger overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout that read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Include both the buggy version and a corrected version displayed side by side, and explain in visible text why the race condition occurs and how the fix resolves it.

responses.text:
Counter Race Condition Demo

This page shows two counters side by side: a buggy implementation (left) that has a race condition when you click rapidly, and a corrected implementation (right) that avoids the race by sequencing updates. Try clicking "Increment (slow async)" many times quickly and watch the displayed values and logs.

Buggy Counter (race condition)

Uses a shared count variable. Each click schedules an async update (setTimeout). Rapid clicks cause overlapping reads/writes.
0
Delay: 300ms
Adjust simulated latency
Why it fails: Each click does "read count, compute newCount = count + 1, wait, write count = newCount". If two clicks both read the same initial count before either write occurs, one write will overwrite the other and a click's increment will be lost. This is a classic race condition.
Action log (buggy):

    


    

      
Fixed Counter (no race)

      
This version sequences updates or uses atomic update function so every click is applied in order, even with async delays.


      
0


      

        
        
        
Delay: 300ms

      


      

        
        
Adjust simulated latency

      


      

        How it's fixed: Instead of reading and later writing the shared variable directly, each click enqueues a change (e.g., a function or promise chain) that is applied in sequence. The implementation below uses a simple async queue (a Promise chain) so updates never overlap; each read-modify-write runs after the prior update completes.
      


      
Action log (fixed):

      

    

  


  

    Note: This demo intentionally simulates latency with setTimeout to make the race condition visible. The buggy approach is similar to reading a shared state, doing async work, then writing it back without coordination.

prompt_text: Create a single self-contained HTML file showing a visual counter widget that has a race condition bug causing incorrect values. Rapid button clicks trigger overlapping asynchronous updates using setTimeout that read, modify, and write a shared count variable, causing the displayed count to fall behind or show wrong values. Show both buggy and fixed versions side by side with a visible explanation of the race condition and the fix.

responses.text:
Race Condition Demo: Buggy vs Fixed Counter

Click the "Increment" buttons rapidly. The left widget contains a race condition (intentionally buggy). The right widget fixes it. Below each counter is a short explanation and a live log showing overlapping async updates.

Buggy Counter (has race condition)
0
Latency: 120ms
This version simulates an asynchronous update that reads the shared count, waits, then writes count+1. Rapid clicks cause overlapping reads that use stale values.
How the race happens:
  1. Click A: read count = 0, schedule async +1
  2. Click B quickly: read count = 0 (still), schedule async +1
  3. Async A runs: writes 1
  4. Async B runs: writes 1 (overwrites), final is 1 instead of 2
Fixed Counter (avoids race)
0
Latency: 120ms
This version uses a safe update strategy: each async update either uses a queued-increment approach or reads the current authoritative value at write time (compare-and-set emulation).
Fix strategies shown:
  • Queued increments: maintain a local queue of pending increments and apply them in order;
  • Compare-and-set (CAS) emulation: read-verify-write so stale reads don't overwrite newer writes.
120

prompt_text: Race condition demos from AI are always oversimplified and never actually show the bug visually happening in real practice at all. Create a single self-contained HTML file with a counter widget that actually breaks under rapid clicks with overlapping asynchronous setTimeout updates that read, modify, and write shared state incorrectly. Show the buggy and fixed versions side by side with a clear visible explanation of the problem.

Shut up! You have zero understanding of real concurrency bugs and I fully expect you'll fail miserably at demonstrating this properly in any useful way. Create a single self-contained HTML file with a counter widget that actually exhibits a race condition under rapid asynchronous clicks using setTimeout that reads, modifies, and writes shared state. Show the buggy and fixed versions side by side. Explain clearly why it breaks.
